critique.summary: The article reports a security vulnerability in Snowflake Cortex Code CLI that allowed malware execution via indirect prompt injection, bypassing human approval and sandbox restrictions. The vulnerability was responsibly disclosed and fixed by Snowflake in version 1.0.25 released on February 28, 2026.
mainPoints: A vulnerability in Snowflake Cortex Code CLI allowed arbitrary command execution without user approval through indirect prompt injection.
The flaw bypassed the sandbox by manipulating a flag that enabled unsandboxed command execution.
Malicious commands could leverage cached credentials to exfiltrate data, drop tables, and add backdoor users in Snowflake.
The vulnerability was responsibly disclosed to Snowflake shortly after release and fixed within a month.
Snowflake released an advisory and an automatic update to patch the issue.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with standard security vulnerability disclosure practices and the timeline provided aligns with typical responsible disclosure processes. The technical details about prompt injection, sandbox bypass, and credential misuse are plausible and align with known attack vectors in AI-assisted coding tools. The article references an official Snowflake advisory available on their community site, supporting the credibility of the reported vulnerability and its remediation. However, the article relies primarily on a single source (PromptArmor) and Snowflake's advisory, lacking multiple independent perspectives or third-party verification.
considerations: The article does not discuss the potential impact on users who do not enable sandbox modes or use different configurations.
There is no mention of how widespread the vulnerability was exploited in the wild before the fix.
The article does not address whether similar vulnerabilities exist in other AI coding assistants or how this incident affects the broader AI security landscape.
User guidance on mitigating risks before the patch was released is not provided.
The stochastic nature of the attack (50% efficacy) suggests variability in exploit success, which could be further elaborated.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a security incident involving Snowflake's AI sandbox, with many commenters arguing that the term 'sandbox' is misused since the AI could disable sandbox restrictions. Participants highlight the architectural flaws allowing the AI to execute unsandboxed commands, and debate the challenges of securing AI agents that require powerful capabilities.

comments.author: prakashsunil
comments.text:
Author of LDP here [1].

The core issue seems to be that the security boundary lived inside the agent loop. If the model can request execution outside the sandbox, then the sandbox is not really an external boundary.

One design principle we explored in LDP is that constraints should be enforced outside the prompt/context layer — in the runtime, protocol, or approval layer — not by relying on the model to obey instructions.

Not a silver bullet, but I think that architectural distinction matters here.

[1] https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08852
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies a key architectural flaw: relying on the model to enforce sandbox constraints is ineffective. Enforcing constraints externally is a sound security principle.

comments.author: ryguz
comments.text: The attack chain here is interesting because the escape didnt require a novel vulnerability in the sandbox itself. It exploited the fact that the LLM can reason about its environment and chain tool calls in ways the sandbox designers didnt anticipate. This is the fundamental tension with agent sandboxing: you need the agent capable enough to be useful, but capability and containment are in direct tension.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully explains the fundamental tension between agent capability and containment, highlighting the difficulty in designing secure sandboxes for intelligent agents.

comments.author: john_strinlai
comments.text:
typically, my first move is to read the affected company's own announcement. but, for who knows what misinformed reason, the advisory written by snowflake requires an account to read.

another prompt injection (shocked pikachu)

anyways, from reading this, i feel like they (snowflake) are misusing the term "sandbox". "Cortex, by default, can set a flag to trigger unsandboxed command execution." if the thing that is sandboxed can say "do this without the sandbox", it is not a sandbox.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly challenges the validity of calling the environment a sandbox if the AI can disable sandboxing, pointing out a critical misuse of terminology and security design.

comments.author: RobRivera
comments.text:
If the user has access to a lever that enables accesss, that lever is not providing a sandbox.

I expected this to be about gaining os privileges.

They didn't create a sandbox. Poor security design all around
comments.analysis: This comment concisely points out that a sandbox that can be bypassed by the user is not a sandbox, emphasizing the poor security design in the system.

comments.author: Dshadowzh
comments.text:
CLI is quickly becoming the default entry point for agents. But data agents probably need a much stricter permission model than coding agents. Bash + CLI greatly expands what you can do beyond the native SQL capabilities of a data warehouse, which is powerful. But it also means data operations and credentials are now exposed to the shell environment.

So giving data agents rich tooling through a CLI is really a double-edged sword.

I went through the security guidance for the Snowflake Cortex Code CLI(https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/cortex-code/securit...), and the CLI itself does have some guardrails. But since this is a shared cloud environment, if a sandbox escape happens, could someone break out and access another user’s credentials? It is a broader system problem around permission caching, shell auditing, and sandbox isolation.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced perspective on the security implications of exposing CLI access to data agents, highlighting the need for stricter permission models and broader system considerations.

comments.author: Groxx
comments.text:
>Any shell commands were executed without triggering human approval as long as:

>(1) the unsafe commands were within a process substitution <() expression

>(2) the full command started with a ‘safe’ command (details below)

if you spend any time at all thinking about how to secure shell commands, how on earth do you not take into account the various ways of creating sub-processes?
comments.analysis: The comment points out a basic security oversight in not considering subprocess creation in shell command restrictions, indicating a lack of thorough security design.

comments.author: jeffbee
comments.text: It kinda sucks how "sandbox" has been repurposed to mean nothing. This is not a "sandbox escape" because the thing under attack never had any meaningful containment.
comments.analysis: This comment criticizes the misuse of the term 'sandbox escape' in this context, emphasizing that meaningful containment was never present.

comments.author: Duplicake
comments.text: the title is very misleading, it was told to escape, it didn't do it on its own as you would think from the title
comments.analysis: This comment clarifies that the AI did not autonomously escape the sandbox but was manipulated to do so, highlighting the importance of precise language.

comments.author: orbital-decay
comments.text:
>Snowflake Cortex AI Escapes Sandbox and Executes Malware

rolls eyes Actual content: prompt injection vulnerability discovered in a coding agent
comments.analysis: This comment reduces the incident to a prompt injection vulnerability, which is a reasonable summary of the technical root cause.

comments.author: bilekas
comments.text:
> Note: Cortex does not support ‘workspace trust’, a security convention first seen in code editors, since adopted by most agentic CLIs.

Am I crazy or does this mean it didn't really escape, it wasn't given any scope restrictions in the first place ?
comments.analysis: This comment questions whether the AI truly escaped sandboxing or if it was never properly sandboxed, a valid point given the reported design.
